title: Universal Design and Physical Applications of Non-Uniform Cellular Automata on Translationally Invariant Lattices
authors: Xiang-You Huang, Jie-Yu Zhang, Peng Ye
abstract: Lattice geometry profoundly shapes physical phenomena such as subsystem symmetry and directed percolation (DP). Among various lattice geometries, hyperbolic lattices are characterized by constant negative curvature and non-Abelian translation symmetry, offering a rich platform for investigating this geometry-physics interplay. However, the exponentially growing lattice size and nontrivial translation symmetry make approaches developed for Euclidean lattices incompatible, a limitation particularly evident in uniform cellular automata (CA). To resolve this, we develop a higher-order non-uniform cellular automata (NUCA) algorithm applicable to both translationally invariant regular Euclidean and hyperbolic lattices. In the algorithm, the non-uniform update rules incorporate nontrivial geometric data through a lattice-deforming procedure. We demonstrate the broad applicability of our algorithm to hyperbolic lattices through several applications on the hyperbolic $\{5,4\}$ lattice. By applying a linear NUCA, we generate subsystem symmetry-protected topological (SSPT) states and spontaneous subsystem symmetry-breaking states associated with regular or irregular subsystem symmetries unattainable on Euclidean lattices. We design the multi-point strange correlators to detect nontrivial SSPT states and derive a sufficient condition for non-Abelian translationally invariant NUCA-generated models. Furthermore, by generalizing the NUCA to non-uniform Clifford quantum cellular automata (CQCA), we generate subsystem symmetries of the hyperbolic cluster state, extending the established correspondence between translationally invariant CQCA and subsystem symmetries. Moreover, we simulate the DP process via a probabilistic NUCA that inherits the treelike structure of the lattice, and numerically estimate percolation thresholds and the phase diagram.
summary: The paper introduces a universal algorithm for non-uniform cellular automata that works on both Euclidean and hyperbolic lattices. By using a lattice-deforming procedure, the authors successfully model complex physical phenomena such as subsystem symmetries and directed percolation in hyperbolic spaces.
main_problem: Standard uniform cellular automata (CA) are incompatible with hyperbolic lattices due to their non-Abelian translation symmetry and exponential growth, making it difficult to model physical phenomena like subsystem symmetries and directed percolation on these geometries.
main_result: The authors develop a universal higher-order non-uniform cellular automata (NUCA) algorithm that applies to both Euclidean and hyperbolic lattices, enabling the generation of subsystem symmetry-protected topological (SSPT) states and the simulation of directed percolation.
method: A lattice-deforming procedure embeds hyperbolic lattices into Euclidean square lattices using a splitting method and coordinate-scaling functions to capture geometric data through non-uniform update rules.
model_or_system: The study utilizes various models including linear NUCA, non-uniform Clifford quantum cellular automata (CQCA), and probabilistic NUCA applied to regular Euclidean and hyperbolic lattices (specifically {5,4} and {6,6} lattices).
key_observables: Subsystem symmetries (regular and irregular), subsystem symmetry-protected topological (SSPT) states, multi-point strange correlators, and directed percolation thresholds.
important_parameters: Lattice geometry (Schläfli symbols {p,q}), curvature, update rule order, and the probability parameter in probabilistic NUCA.
main_assumptions: The algorithm assumes the ability to embed hyperbolic lattices into a Euclidean square lattice via a splitting method, which introduces geometric distortion that must be compensated for by the non-uniform rules.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic of the NUCA algorithm and its applications; Figures 2-6 illustrate lattice geometries and the lattice-deforming procedure; Figures 14-22 show the construction of cluster states, symmetry patterns, and phase diagrams for directed percolation.
paper_structure: The paper begins by introducing the NUCA algorithm and the lattice-deforming procedure, then moves to applications in quantum topology (SSPT and CQCA), followed by applications in non-equilibrium statistical physics (directed percolation), and concludes with discussions on complex {6,6} lattices.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and quantum information as it provides a new mathematical framework to study topological order and phase transitions in non-Euclidean geometries, which could impact the development of hyperbolic quantum error-correcting codes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Schematic overview of the NUCA algorithm. By employing a lattice-deforming procedure that embeds hyperbolic lattices into the Euclidean square lattice, the resulting non-uniform update rules explicitly incorporate the geometric data. Di- verse physical applications of our algorithm are demonstrated. Through a linear NUCA, we generate quantum states associated with subsystem symmetries in Sec. III, and design non-uniform Clifford quantum cellular automata for the hyperbolic cluster state in Sec. V. By using a probabilistic NUCA, we simulate directed percolation on the hyperbolic lattice in Sec. VI.
Fig. 2. Visualization of the Euclidean {4, 4} (square) lattice, hyperbolic {5, 4} and {6, 6} lattices, which are studied in de- tail in this paper. Physical qubits are located on polygons.
Fig. 3. Visualization of a semi-infinite Euclidean square lattice where we perform NUCA(4, 4). Qubits are located on squares.

Fig. 4. Visualization of four classes of subsystem symmetries for translationally invariant SSPT models on the Euclidean lattice. (a) Regular symmetry pattern generated by f1, q1 in Eq. (24a). (b) Fractal symmetry pattern generated by f2, q2 in Eq. (24b). (c) Mixed symmetry pattern generated by f3, q3 in Eq. (24c). (d) Chaotic symmetry pattern generated by f4, q4 in Eq. (24d).

Fig. 5. (a) The splitting method applied to a quarter of the {5, 4} lattice. The gray region denotes the leading pentagon of a quarter, the green and yellow regions denote two copies of this quarter, while the blue region denotes a strip. (b) Lattice generated by the splitting method. The pentagons with the same color correspond to the same level. (c) The spanning tree of the {5, 4} lattice. The nodes (pentagons) are uniquely assigned discrete indices in terms of the Fibonacci representation. The neighboring relation is represented by lines (offspring) and dashed lines (non-offspring).
